worth_it_explanation: A contact list uploaded for Matched Audiences and the data flowing through the Insight Tag are both personal data, so they carry obligations the rest of the account does not. The checks are quick, and they catch the kind of gap that draws a regulator's or a client's attention. Confirming the lawful basis, the retention plan, and the privacy disclosures takes a short review of Campaign Manager, the uploaded lists, and the site's privacy notice. The fix is usually a documentation or scheduling change rather than a rebuild, and it lowers the risk of holding personal data longer than the lawful basis allows or sharing data the privacy notice never disclosed.
intro: Matched Audiences lets an advertiser upload a contact list, and the LinkedIn Insight Tag collects website visit data, so both paths move personal data into LinkedIn for advertising. LinkedIn hashes uploaded email lists in the browser and applies its own retention windows, but the advertiser still owns the lawful basis for using the data and the disclosures that describe it. These checks confirm that uploaded lists have a lawful basis and a refresh or deletion plan, and that the site's privacy notice and processing terms reflect what the Insight Tag and conversions actually share.

audit_checks.title: Uploaded Matched Audience data is not handled against consent and retention rules
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A contact list used for advertising without a documented lawful basis exposes the advertiser to a complaint or a regulator request it cannot answer. Holding the list indefinitely keeps personal data in use past the point the original consent or legitimate interest supports, which widens the exposure with every month the list sits unrefreshed.

educate.base:
A contact list uploaded for LinkedIn Matched Audiences is personal data. The email addresses identify real people, so using them to target ads needs a lawful basis (e.g., consent or a legitimate interest the advertiser can defend) and a plan for how long the list stays in use. LinkedIn hashes the emails in the browser before they reach its data center, but hashing protects the data in transit. It does not give the advertiser a lawful basis to use the list in the first place.

LinkedIn applies its own retention windows to uploaded data. A contact list file is deleted within 30 days, and an audience built from a list is deleted within 90 days if it is not edited or used in an active campaign, per LinkedIn's data collection and storage documentation. Those windows govern LinkedIn's copy of the data. They do not cover the source list the advertiser keeps, the lawful basis behind it, or whether a contact who withdrew consent is still on the file that gets re-uploaded.

You may want to confirm a few things about each uploaded list:
  • A documented lawful basis exists for using the contacts in advertising
  • A refresh or suppression process removes contacts who opted out before the next upload
  • A deletion or re-upload schedule keeps the list from being held and used indefinitely
LinkedIn also notes that the advertiser is responsible for having the legal right to provide any personal data, including in hashed form, for advertising. You may want to treat the uploaded list as data the advertiser is accountable for, not data that becomes LinkedIn's problem once it is hashed.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a considered-purchase or B2B store, a customer list pulled from order history may carry a lawful basis for fulfilment rather than advertising, so the two purposes may not transfer. You may want to confirm the basis covers advertising use before the list is uploaded for Matched Audiences.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-capture site, the contacts often come from gated content or demo forms, so the consent language on those forms determines whether the emails can be used for LinkedIn advertising. You may want to confirm the form's consent covers advertising and that contacts who opted out are suppressed before each upload.

learn_more.source_title: Data collection and storage for LinkedIn Matched Audiences

investigate.intro: In Campaign Manager, review each uploaded contact list against a documented lawful basis and a refresh or deletion schedule.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Open Campaign Manager, select the ad account, and go to 'Plan', then 'Audiences' to see the saved audiences.
Identify the audiences built from an uploaded contact list (the list-upload source is shown for each), and note their creation or last-edited dates.
For each list-based audience, check with the team whether a documented lawful basis exists for advertising use and whether a suppression step removes opted-out contacts before re-upload.

Note any audience that has not been edited or used recently, given that LinkedIn deletes an unused list-based audience within 90 days, and confirm whether the source list is on a refresh or deletion schedule.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per uploaded contact list, whether a documented lawful basis for advertising exists, whether opted-out contacts are suppressed before upload, and whether a refresh or deletion schedule is in place. A list with a documented basis, suppression, and a schedule passes. A list with no documented basis, no suppression of opt-outs, or an indefinite hold is a fail recorded with the list or audience name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the lawful basis, suppression, and retention status for each uploaded contact list above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the uploaded contact lists used for {{site_name}}'s Matched Audiences are not handled against a documented lawful basis and retention plan. The email addresses are personal data, so using them for advertising needs a defensible basis and a schedule that removes opted-out contacts and prevents an indefinite hold. Without that, {{site_name}} risks using contacts past the point the original consent supports and cannot answer a complaint or a regulator request about the lists. {{voice_recommend}} documenting the lawful basis for each list, adding a suppression step that removes opt-outs before every re-upload, and setting a refresh or deletion schedule so lists are not held longer than the basis allows.

audit_checks.title: Data shared through the Insight Tag and conversions is not reviewed against privacy commitments
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the Insight Tag and conversion data move personal data to LinkedIn that the privacy notice never describes, the site's disclosures no longer match what it actually does. That mismatch is the kind of detail that surfaces in a consumer complaint or a data subject request and is hard to defend after the fact.

educate.base:
The LinkedIn Insight Tag is JavaScript added to a site to enable retargeting and conversion reporting. It drops a LinkedIn cookie and collects metadata such as the page URL, referrer, Internet Protocol (IP) address, device and browser details, and a timestamp. Conversion events the advertiser configures send their own data back to LinkedIn. Both flows move personal data from the site to LinkedIn, so the site's privacy notice and the data processing terms with LinkedIn should describe what is actually shared.

LinkedIn handles its copy of Insight Tag data on a set schedule. It truncates or hashes IP addresses and removes members' direct identifiers within seven days, then deletes the remaining pseudonymized data within 180 days, per the LinkedIn Insight Tag FAQs. That covers LinkedIn's processing. It does not cover whether the site disclosed the Insight Tag to visitors, whether a consent banner gates the tag where consent is required, or whether the site avoids placing the tag on pages LinkedIn restricts.

LinkedIn also restricts where the Insight Tag may run. It states the tag should not be installed on pages that collect or contain sensitive data, e.g., pages offering specific health or financial services, so a tag firing site-wide can reach pages it is not permitted on.

You may want to confirm a few things about the shared data:
  • The privacy notice describes the Insight Tag, the LinkedIn cookie, and the data the conversions send
  • Consent is captured where the law requires it before the tag fires
  • The tag is excluded from sensitive pages LinkedIn does not allow it on
  • The data processing terms with LinkedIn are accepted and cover the data actually shared
You may want to compare the site's disclosures against what the tag and conversions transmit, rather than assuming the standard privacy notice already covers them.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a considered-purchase or B2B store, conversion events often send order or checkout data, so the privacy notice should describe what those events share with LinkedIn. You may want to confirm the tag is kept off account-management or payment pages, which can fall under LinkedIn's sensitive-data restriction.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-capture site, the conversion fires on form submission, so the privacy notice should disclose that a completed lead is shared with LinkedIn for measurement. You may want to confirm consent is captured before the Insight Tag loads where the law requires it, given that the form pages are where personal data is entered.

investigate.intro: Confirm that the site's privacy notice and consent setup describe the Insight Tag and conversion data, and that the tag is kept off restricted pages.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Open Campaign Manager, select the ad account, and go to 'Analyze', then 'Insight Tag' to confirm the tag is installed and which domains report data.
Review the conversions under 'Analyze', then 'Conversion tracking' to see which events are configured and what each one fires on.
Open the live site and read its privacy notice and cookie policy. Confirm they name the LinkedIn Insight Tag, the LinkedIn cookie, and the data the configured conversions send.
Check that the data processing terms with LinkedIn are accepted for the account and cover the data the tag and conversions actually share.

browser_devtools.label: Browser DevTools
steps: Open the site in a browser, open DevTools, and watch the Network panel for requests to px.ads.linkedin.com as you load pages.
Confirm the Insight Tag fires only where intended, and check that it does not load on pages LinkedIn restricts, e.g., health or financial account pages.
Where consent is required, confirm the tag does not fire until the visitor accepts, rather than loading before the consent banner is answered.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the privacy notice describes the Insight Tag and conversion data, whether consent gates the tag where required, whether the tag is excluded from restricted pages, and whether the processing terms cover the shared data. Disclosures that match what the tag and conversions transmit, with consent and page restrictions honored, pass. A privacy notice that omits the Insight Tag, a tag that fires before consent, or a tag on a restricted page is a fail recorded with the specific gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the privacy notice, consent, page-restriction, and processing-terms status above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the data {{site_name}} shares through the LinkedIn Insight Tag and its conversions is not reviewed against the site's privacy commitments. The tag and conversion events move personal data to LinkedIn, e.g., the page URL, IP address, and configured conversion details, yet the privacy notice and processing terms do not fully describe what is shared. That leaves the site's disclosures out of step with what it actually does, which is hard to defend in a consumer complaint or a data subject request. {{voice_recommend}} updating the privacy notice to describe the Insight Tag and the conversion data, confirming consent gates the tag where the law requires it, keeping the tag off the sensitive pages LinkedIn restricts, and accepting processing terms that cover the data actually shared.
